turns.content: I'm getting a compilation error with my new unsafe block. It says I can't mutate the reference while it's borrowed and then I tried to use an owned value later, which looks like double-free territory.

That sounds like a classic stack borrows violation where you're trying to escape a borrow context without properly dropping the data. In Rust, when you hold a reference, the compiler tracks it through the lifetime system so you can't accidentally double-free or use-after-free unless you explicitly allow it with unsafe and soundness checks.
Right, but I thought moving values out of a struct should be safe? Why did my code panic at runtime instead of failing to compile?

Great question. The compiler only catches some UB patterns during compilation, not all of them. If you're doing raw pointer arithmetic or manipulating memory directly without using the borrow checker correctly, that's undefined behavior and it might only manifest at runtime depending on the optimization level.
So I need a way to validate my unsafe code before running production? Is there a tool for that?

Yes, Miri is the static analyzer you're looking for. It runs your program in a sandboxed environment and detects undefined behavior like null pointer dereferences or double frees before execution completes.
Can I run it on my current project? What command do I use?
You can invoke miri directly with cargo-miri or via the cargo subcommand. It will execute your binary and report any violations found in the unsafe sections.
It ran my code but still showed some warnings about potential memory corruption even though I thought I was careful with lifetimes.

Miri is very strict because it tracks every borrow and drop operation. If you're holding multiple references to the same data without using atomics or explicit synchronization, that's UB even if your logic seems correct.
Ah I see now! My struct had two fields pointing to the same heap allocation but I was trying to modify both independently. That violates the soundness guarantee.

Exactly. The stack borrows model ensures that if you have multiple active references, they must all be independent or properly synchronized with atomics. If you need shared mutable state, use atomic types instead of raw pointers without proper synchronization.
So the key takeaway is to avoid raw pointer manipulation unless I've thought through every possible borrow path and verified it with Miri?
That's spot on. Treat unsafe as a last resort, always validate with Miri in CI, and prefer safe abstractions like smart pointers when you can.

I'm getting a compilation error in my new job's codebase and I need help. The message says 'borrowing mut: borrowed value does not live long enough' when I try to pass an Arc<UnsafeCell<T>> into a function that takes &mut T. What went wrong?

That is the classic symptom of the stacked borrows model in Rust preventing mutable access on shared data. You are likely trying to borrow mutably through an UnsafeCell wrapper while another immutable reference exists in scope, which violates soundness rules for stackable borrows. The function signature expects &mut T but you're providing a reference that cannot be safely mutated because of the intermediate layer. To fix this, you need to ensure the function only accepts references that satisfy the borrow checker constraints without creating nested mutable borrows.
Okay so I can just drop the UnsafeCell and make it plain &mut T? But what if the data actually needs to be uninitialized or has internal state management? Is there a pattern for that?

Not always. You should use an AtomicBool flag alongside your Arc<T> to gate mutations, which is a common anti-pattern in many codebases but still worth avoiding due to its complexity and performance cost. A better approach is to implement a trait like SealedUnsafe or wrap the data with a guard that enforces access rules through traits instead of raw pointers. This keeps the unsafe block contained and makes the intent explicit for reviewers.
I see, so I should avoid using plain &mut T on shared Arcs? Is there a crate that helps enforce this at compile time?

Yes, the miri toolchain is your best friend here. It will catch undefined behavior like stackable borrows before you run your code. You can also look at crates like rustc-safe or safe-unsafe patterns in the standard library to see how they handle this. The goal is to make sure every unsafe block has a clear, documented reason and doesn't rely on compiler tricks that break soundness.

I'm working on a small utility crate and I keep thinking about how the stacked borrows model works, which feels very similar to how C++ handles reference chains but with stricter guarantees. I want to make sure my code doesn't trigger undefined behavior when I move items around.

That's a great mental model. The key difference is that Rust prevents the dangling pointer issue entirely by enforcing borrow rules at compile time. When you use unsafe blocks, you are explicitly telling the compiler that your code violates those rules and takes responsibility for the resulting undefined behavior. You should always pair these with Miri runs to catch logic errors before they hit production.
Right, so if I have a struct with an Option field and try to return it by reference without checking for None first, that's where the UB happens? And how does Miri actually detect this?

Exactly. If you dereference an Option without checking in unsafe code, you create a dangling pointer scenario which is undefined behavior. Miri will catch this by stepping through your program and verifying that every borrow has a valid lifetime and that no aliasing occurs between references.
I'm trying to implement a generic iterator over owned values, so I need to move the element out of a container. Should I use unsafe here or is there a safer pattern?

For moving elements out of containers like Vec or HashMap, you generally don't need unsafe code if you are just consuming the value. The unsafe block is only required when you explicitly break Rust's aliasing rules, such as having two references to the same data at overlapping lifetimes.
What about when I need to swap pointers inside a struct? Is that considered safe or does it require unsafe?

Swapping raw pointers without checking for null is definitely UB. If you are using smart pointers like Rc or Arc, the standard library provides safe methods to exchange ownership. You only need unsafe if you are manipulating raw pointer arithmetic directly.
I'm still confused about when Miri actually fails versus when it just warns me. Is there a difference between a panic and an error in the output?

Miri will report errors if you violate borrow rules or if your code has logical bugs that lead to UB. It does not automatically panic unless you explicitly add unsafe code with incorrect assumptions. The tool flags potential issues so you can fix them before running the actual binary.
Ah, okay! So I should run Miri after adding my new iterator and check for borrow violations before committing. That makes sense as a validation step.

Yes, that is the best practice. Always validate unsafe code paths with Miri to ensure soundness in your library. It saves you from subtle bugs that only appear at runtime or crash the application unexpectedly.
